conditions: Each subcommand owns a clearly namespaced section so keys never collide and ownership is obvious.
The loader applies a fixed precedence (built-in defaults < file < env/flags) and validates the whole document against one schema after a single parse.
Unknown or future sections are preserved/ignored rather than rejected, so adding the 7th..12th command is additive and never a breaking change.
A documented top-level schema version field exists so evolution is explicit without forcing migrations.
completed.keyRisk: A single file optimizes the loader and the source-of-truth invariant but can become awkward to hand-edit and merge as it grows toward 12 sections; mitigation is strict namespacing, schema validation with good error messages, and the fact that one-to-many is a safe additive split later while many-to-one is the painful, breaking direction.
completed.position: Keep all configuration in a single hand-editable file with one namespaced section per subcommand (e.g. a [commands.<name>] table), loaded by one read-parse-merge-with-defaults pass.
wouldChangeIf: Subcommands turn out to need independent lifecycles (separately versioned, separately shipped/installed as plugins, or per-command secrets with different trust boundaries).
The file is realistically projected to exceed roughly a few hundred lines such that hand-editing one file becomes error-prone for users.
Two or more commands need genuinely conflicting global keys that cannot be disambiguated by namespacing.
Evidence shows users will most often edit exactly one command in isolation and per-file diffs/review materially help that workflow.
